content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To     : Barbara Milman                                    Date :April 8, 1983

                                                           No. M.83-294


From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Janis McLean

Subject: Coastal Commission


General Information:

        The Coastal Commission is responsible for the coastal law.
(Pub. Resoures Code Sections 30300, et seq.) It replaced the
Coastal Zone Conservation Commission (many of the older cases
refer to the name). The Coastal Commission does the following
(this list is not inclusive):

        1.     Reviews and certifies local coastal programs, land use
               plans, zoning ordinances, zoning district maps and
               other implementing actions;

        2      Designates sensitive coastal resources areas;

        3.     May prohibit development in an area that does not have
               a local coastal program;

        4.     Reviews local programs to see if they are being
               properly implemented and makes recommendations for
               changes;

        5.     Issues some types of permits;

        6.     Handles appeals from decisions by local governments
               (de novo hearing); and

        7.     Prepares local programs when requested to by local
               governments.

Statute:

        Public Resources Code Section 30801 provides that:

               Any aggrieved person shall have a right to judicial
        review of any decision or action of the commission or of a

Barbara Milman
April 7, 1983
Page 2


regional commission by filing a petition for writ of mandate
in accordance with the provisions of Section 1094.5 of the
Code of Civil Procedure, within 60 days after such decision
or action has become final....

(Note: The repealed Coastal Act permitted an applicant to avail
himself of a writ of mandate, but it did not specify a CCP
section number.)

Cases:

City of Chula Vista v. Superior Court of San Diego County
(1982), 133 CA 3d 472 (4th Dist.)

The Coastal Commission refused to approve a city's proposed
local coastal program. The court reviewed this refusal using the
substantial evidence test. The court stated that:

1. The Coastal Act states that Commission decisions are
reviewable under Section 1094.5, but this is not
determinative of whether the Commission is acting in a
quasi-judicial or quasi-legislative manner on the
decision in question.

2. The determination of whether a particular function
being reviewed is quasi-judicial or quasi-legislative
is for the court to decide. It also decides whether a
fundamental right is involved and then, from these
determinations, it decides what type of review is
authorized.

3. The certification of a local coastal plan has
attributes of both types of functions.

a. The approval of a land use plan or zoning package
is like the "undisputably legislative function" of
enacting zoning ordinances, in that the result of
the process will be the promulgation of general
development criteria and land use regulations for a
large physical area affecting many members of the
public. (Selby Realty Co. v. City of San
Benaventura (1973), 10 Cal.3d 110, 118; Karlson v.
City of Camarillo (1980), 100 CA 3d 789, 799.)

b. It is also like a judicial function because general
criteria (the statewide standards of the Act) are
applied in an individual instance (the local

Barbara Milman
April 7, 1983
Page 3


                    program in question) to make an individual
                    determination of conformance or nonconformance.
                    (Applying a general law to an existing set of
                    facts.)

            c.  The procedures mandated by the Act share common
                features with the judicial process, such as the
                holding of hearings and the taking of testimony to
                adjudicate individual issues.  (However, it is also
                like the legislative process because the Commission
                gathers evidence independently; formulates general
                standards from the evidence; employs its own
                experts; and considers external facts not brought
                before it by the parties.)

            d.  Most significant, the Commission does not originate
                any land use rules and regulations. It approves or
                disapproves the plan, but it doesn't draft
                anything. This makes the function closer to an
                adjudicatory function and the standard of reivew is
                substantial evidence.

        4.  Quote: "The issue of whether a function is legislative
            or judicial is sufficiently complex to baffle the most
            sophisticated courts which routinely must deal with the
            issue."

Patterson v. Central Coast Regional Commission (1976), 58 CA 3d
833, 840 (1st Dist.). The court held that the permit process is
clearly adjudicatory because it involves the application of
existing rules to a specific set of existing facts. Section
1094.5 was used for the review.

        1.  In Patterson,there is a discussion of the differences
            between review under traditional mandamus and review
            under administrative mandamus.

        2.  The court noted that "the distinction between a
            quasi-legislative and adjudicatory determination is
            sometimes difficult to draw.  [W]hether a particular
            action of an administrative agency is quasi-legislative
            or adjudicatory depends on the nature of the function
            delegated by the enabling statute, rather than the
            specific procedures followed."  (p.840)

        3.  The court stated, "Whenever an act undertakes to
            determine a question of right or obligation, or of

Barbara Milman
April 7, 1983
Page 4

                property, as the foundation upon which it proceeds,
                such act is to that extent a judicial one, and not the
                proper exercise of legislative functions." (pp.
                840-841) (cites omitted)

     4.   The court also stated that the procedural
          characteristics of the administrative process are not
          necessarily determinative of the classification of the
          activity (cites omitted). Nor is the breadth or
          narrowness of the agency's discretion controlling (cite
          omitted).

Cases with no discussion about the use of CCP Section
1094.5:

     Aries Development Co. v. Calif. Coastal Zone Conservation
Comm. (1975), 48 CA 3d 534 (4th Dist.). The court reviewed the
Commission's denial of an exemption from the Act's requirements.
Section 1094.5 was used. (See also Transcentury Properties, Inc.
v. State of Calif. (1974), 41 CA 3d 835 (1st Dist.)).

     City of San Diego v. Calif. Coastal Comm. (1981), 110 CA 3d
228 (4th Dist.). The court reviewed the denial of a permit.
Section 1094.5 was used. (See also Davis v. Calif. Coastal Comm.
(1976), 57 CA 3d 700; State of Calif. v. Sup. Court (1974), 12
Cal. 3d 237 (1st Dist.).

sections.question_synthetic: This document does not pose a specific legal question but rather provides general information about the Coastal Commission's jurisdiction, functions, and judicial review standards applicable to its decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is informational in nature rather than a responsive advice letter. No traditional Q&A sections present. Contains general information about Coastal Commission functions and substantial case law citations. Date discrepancy noted (April 7 and April 8, 1983). This appears to be background research or informational memo rather than FPPC advice responding to a specific requester question.

embedding.summary: This is an informational memorandum from FPPC staff member Janis McLean to Barbara Milman providing background information on the California Coastal Commission, its statutory authority under Public Resources Code Section 30801, and case law discussing judicial review standards for Coastal Commission decisions under Code of Civil Procedure Section 1094.5.
